1: Chapter 3 returns to the direct “my son (bᵉnî)” address (cf. 1:8; 2:1). “My instruction (tôrātî)” — tôrāh here is parental teaching, but the word is the same as the Mosaic Torah; the wisdom teacher’s instruction participates in the same authority structure. “Let your heart hold on (yiṣṣōr) to my commands” — nāṣar (to guard, keep carefully) is the same verb used of God’s keeping steadfast love (Exod 34:7) and the Psalmist keeping the law (Ps 119:2).
2: “They will add to you a long life (ʾōrek yāmîm), many years of living (šᵉnôt ḥayyîm), and peace (šālôm).” Long life and peace are the standard covenant-blessing terms: ʾōrek yāmîm appears in the Aaronic blessing-promise context (Ps 21:4; 23:6); šālôm is the comprehensive covenant wellbeing (completeness, flourishing, not just absence of conflict). These are the outcomes of wisdom that motivate the son to listen.
3: “Let steadfast love (ḥesed) and faithfulness (ʾĕmet) never leave you.” The paired covenant attributes ḥesed wĕʾĕmet are the core description of YHWH’s character at Sinai (Exod 34:6–7) and appear throughout the Psalms (Ps 25:10; 40:10–11; 85:10). The student is called to embody the divine character. “Wear them like a necklace (qašrēm ʾal-gargᵉrōtekā)” — bind them on your throat (cf. 1:9; 6:21; Deut 6:8). “Inscribe them on your heart (kātᵉbēm ʾal-lûaḥ libbekā)” — the language of Deut 6:6 (“these words... shall be on your heart”) and Jer 31:33 (the new covenant written on hearts).
4: “You will find favor (ḥēn) and a good reputation (śēkel-ṭôb) in the eyes of God and people (bᵉʿênê ʾĕlōhîm wᵉʾādām).” The dual approval — divine and human — recurs in describing Joseph (Gen 39:3–4, 21), Samuel (1 Sam 2:26), and Jesus (Luke 2:52). Śēkel (prudence, good sense, insight) is the practical wisdom that makes one well-regarded (cf. 1 Sam 25:3).
5: “Trust in the LORD (beṭaḥ ʾel-YHWH) with all your heart (bᵉkol-libbekā).” One of the most cited verses in Proverbs. Bāṭaḥ (to trust, be secure) is a covenantal security term (cf. 1:33; Ps 22:4–5; Isa 26:3–4). “Do not depend on your own insight (ʾal-tiššāʿēn ʾel-bînātekā)” — šāʿan (to lean on, rely on) occurs with negative force in Isa 31:1 (“who rely on horses”) and Ezek 29:7. The contrast is not between reason and faith but between self-sufficient reasoning and God-dependent wisdom.
6: “Acknowledge him (dāʿēhû) in everything you do (bᵉkol-dᵉrākekā), and he will direct your steps (yᵉyaššēr ʾorḥōtekā).” Yādaʾ (to know) here is covenantal knowing — to acknowledge, reckon with, give full weight to (cf. 1 Sam 2:12; Hos 4:1). The promise is that God will “make straight (yāšar, piel)” the paths — removing obstacles, straightening the crooked way.
7: “Do not think of yourself as wise in your own eyes (ʾal-tᵉhî ḥākām bᵉʿênekā).” The precise phrasing recurs in 26:12 (“do you see a person wise in their own eyes? There is more hope for a fool”) and Isa 5:21 (“woe to those who are wise in their own eyes”). Self-assessed wisdom is the root of arrogant folly. “Fear the LORD and turn from evil” — the same pairing as the programmatic verse (1:7 + 3:7b) grounds the ethical turn in covenant piety.
8: “This will bring health (ripʾût) to your whole body (lᵉšârekkā) and strength (šiqwî) to your bones.” Šōr (navel, or possibly the body’s whole frame — the word is rare) is sometimes translated “navel” or “body.” Šiqwî is also rare — “refreshment, moisture” — suggesting the bones being moistened rather than dry (cf. Prov 17:22; Job 21:24). The physical dimension of wisdom’s blessings is consistent throughout Proverbs: wisdom is embodied.
9: “Honor the LORD with your wealth (hôn)” and “the first portion (rēʾšît) of all you produce.” Rēʾšît (firstfruits, best portion — the same word as “beginning” in 1:7) links wisdom’s foundation to the concrete act of giving God the first and best of one’s produce. The firstfruits legislation (Exod 23:19; Deut 26:1–11) is here applied to income broadly: the principle generalizes beyond agriculture.
10: “Your barns (ʾᵃsāmekā) will overflow with abundance (šābāʿ) and your wine vats (yᵉqābêkā) will brim with new wine (tîrôš).” The agricultural blessing-language of Deuteronomy (Deut 28:8; Mal 3:10) — full storehouses and overflowing wine vats as the material expression of divine favor. Tîrôš is the fresh-pressed grape juice / must, one of the grain-wine-oil triad of covenant prosperity.
11: “Do not reject (ʾal-timʾas) the LORD’s discipline (mûsar-YHWH)” and “do not resent (wᵉʾal-tāqōṣ) his correction (tôkaḥtô).” The strong imperatives guard against two reactions to hardship: rejecting (treating as contemptible) and resenting (feeling weary and disgusted — qûṣ means to feel loathing, cf. Gen 27:46; Exod 1:12). Discipline and correction are not signs of divine rejection but of divine engagement.
12: “For the LORD corrects (yôkîaḥ) those he loves (yᵉʾāhab), just as a father disciplines the son he takes delight in (yirṣeh).” Yakach (hiphil: to reprove, correct, argue a case) carries a forensic dimension — it is the word for presenting evidence and making one accountable (cf. Job 9:33; 16:21; Isa 2:4). God’s correction is the action of a parent who takes pleasure (rāṣāh) in the son’s flourishing. This verse is directly quoted in Heb 12:6.
13: “How happy (ʾašrê) is the person who finds wisdom (māṣāʾ ḥokmāh), the one who gains understanding (yāpîq tᵉbûnāh).” The ʾašrê (beatitude) formula — “O the happiness of the one who...” — is used extensively in Psalms (Ps 1:1; 2:12; 34:8) and opens the Sermon on the Mount (Matt 5:3–11). It is exclamatory, not conditional — declaring the blessed state of the one who already possesses wisdom.
14: “Her return (sāḥrāh) is better than the return on silver (kesef), and her profit (tᵉbûʾātāh) better than fine gold (ḥārûṣ).” Sāḥar is trading, commerce — wisdom measured as a return on investment. Ḥārûṣ (fine, mined gold) is the most refined and valuable metal, the standard of ultimate value (cf. Ps 19:10; Prov 8:10–11). The merchant-language deliberately transposes wisdom into economic categories to communicate her surpassing value.
15: “She is more valuable than precious gems (mippᵉnînîm).” Pᵉnînîm (probably rubies or corals — the exact stone is debated) represents the most precious of luxury goods. “Nothing you could want (kol-ḥᵃpāṣekā) measures up to her.” Ḥāpēṣ (to delight in, desire) — the strongest human wanting. The verse recurs almost verbatim in 8:11 in Wisdom’s self-speech.
16: “In her right hand is long life (ʾōrek yāmîm); in her left hand are wealth (ʿōšer) and honor (kābôd).” The two-handed image is a visual anthropomorphism: wisdom as a figure who holds gifts. The right hand held the greater gift in OT symbolism (Gen 48:14; Ps 110:1). Long life ranks above wealth and honor — wisdom’s hierarchy of values. All three are explicitly the gifts Solomon received for asking wisdom rather than wealth (1 Kgs 3:11–14).
17: “Her ways (dᵉrākêhā) are pleasant (dᵉrākê-nōʿam) and all her paths (nᵉtîbôtêhā) lead to peace (šālôm).” Nōʿam (pleasantness, loveliness, delightfulness — root of the name Naomi, Ruth 1:20) characterizes wisdom’s way as intrinsically good to travel. Šālôm (comprehensive wellbeing) is the destination, not just the absence of conflict. The paths of wisdom are categorically different from the twisted paths of the wicked (2:13–15).
18: “She is a tree of life (ʿēṣ ḥayyîm) to those who grasp her.” The ʿēṣ ḥayyîm (tree of life) recalls Gen 2:9; 3:22–24 — the source of immortality from which humanity was cut off after the fall. Proverbs applies the image to wisdom: she restores access to what sin forfeited. The tree of life recurs in 11:30; 13:12; 15:4 and finally in Rev 22:2. “Those who hold on to her (tōmᵉkêhā) are called blessed (mᵉʾuššār)” — the participle of the beatitude-root.
19: “By wisdom (bᵉḥokmāh) the LORD laid the foundation (yāsad) of the earth; by understanding (bîn) he set the heavens in place.” The cosmological grounding of wisdom: it was the instrument of creation (cf. 8:22–31; Job 28:23–27; Ps 104:24). The same three wisdom-terms — ḥokmāh, tᵉbûnāh, daʿat — appear in vv19–20 as the tools of creation, tying the student’s pursuit of wisdom to participation in the order built into creation itself.
20: “Through his knowledge (bᵉdaʿtô) the deep waters (tᵉhōmôt) opened up and the clouds let fall their dew (ʾᵃbāpîm yirᵃʿᵃpû-ṭāl).” Tᵉhôm (the deep, the primeval ocean) is the chaos-water of Gen 1:2 that God separated at creation. The “falling of dew” may allude to the second-day separation of waters and the natural water cycle. Creation is the domain of the wisdom the student is being invited to pursue.
21: “My son, keep these in view (ʾal-yālûzû mêʿênekā = do not let them escape from your eyes); hold on to sound wisdom (tûšîyyāh) and discernment (mᵉzimmāh).” Lûz (to turn aside, deviate — as in 2:15) is used negatively: do not let the teaching deviate from your sight. Tûšîyyāh (sound, effective wisdom — 2:7) and mᵉzimmāh (prudent planning — 2:11) return from ch2 to form a bracket.
22: “They will bring life (ḥayyîm) to your soul (napsᵉkā) and beauty (ḥēn) to your neck.” The same jewelry-and-garland imagery as 1:9; beauty to the neck denotes social honor. Nepeš (throat, soul, self) is where life is centered; wisdom gives life to the whole person.
23: “Then you will go on your way (derkᵉkā) in security (lābeṭaḥ), without stumbling (wᵉraglᵉkā lōʾ-tāgôp).” The journey-security promise — the way (derek) is safe for the wisdom-keeper. Nāgap (to stumble, trip) is used of military defeat and stumbling over obstacles (Ps 91:12; Isa 8:14). The protected path is the opposite of the crooked paths of ch2.
24: “When you lie down (tiškeab), you will not be afraid (lōʾ-tîrāʾ); your sleep (šᵉnātekā) will be peaceful (wᵉʿārebāh).” Peaceful sleep as the sign of security appears also in Ps 4:8; Lev 26:6 (the covenant-blessing land where “you will lie down with no one to make you afraid”). ʿāreb (pleasant, sweet) — the taste of undisturbed rest that only comes with cleared conscience and divine protection.
25: “Do not fear sudden catastrophe (paḥad pittʾōm) or the destruction (šōʾāh) that falls on the wicked.” Paḥad pittʾōm (sudden dread) is the unexpected disaster that cannot be planned against (cf. Ps 91:5; Job 22:10). Šōʾāh (desolation, wasteland — from a root meaning to be devastated) is the most complete destruction-word in Hebrew, used of the cosmic judgment in Zeph 1:15. The righteous need not fear what overtakes the wicked.
26: “For the LORD will be your security (ûbᵉkîslᵉkā)” — literally “and in your side/hip will be the LORD” — kesil (hip, side, confidence; cf. Job 31:24 where it means “confidence”). “He will keep your foot (raglᵉkā) from every snare (milhāqed).” The LORD as the guardian of the foot is the language of Ps 121:3; 91:12. The snare (mōqēš variant: milhāqed = trap/net) is the enemy’s hidden trap.
27: “Do not withhold (ʾal-timnaʿ) good (ṭôb) from those who deserve it (mibbᵉʿālāyw = from its owners)” — baʿal (owner, master) applied to “good”: those to whom good-doing properly belongs as their due. The ethical demand now turns outward to neighbor ethics. “When you have the ability (bᵉhyôt lᵉʾēl yādᵉkā = when it is in the power of your hand)” — the qualification is not ability but will.
28: “Do not tell your neighbor, ‘Come back later — I’ll help you tomorrow,’ when you can help now (wᵉyēš-ʾittāk = and it is with you).” The deferral-of-good is its own form of withholding. The neighbor (rēaʿ) is the one with whom you share life and community. The command presupposes the capacity to help — the sin is procrastination, not poverty. Jas 2:15–16 applies this principle to the Christian community.
29: “Do not plot (tāḥarōš) harm (rāʿāh) against your neighbor, who lives beside you in trust (lābeṭaḥ).” Ḥāraš (to plow, devise, plot — Ps 10:2; Mic 2:1) — the agricultural metaphor of plowing transferred to the deliberate scheming of harm. “Who lives beside you in trust (bōṭēaḥ)” — the neighbor’s security (bāṭaḥ) is based on trust; plotting destroys the social fabric of the community built on that trust.
30: “Do not pick a fight (ʾal-tārîb) without cause (ḥinnām) with someone who has done you no harm (lōʾ-gᵉmālaʿkā rāʿāh).” Rîb (lawsuit, quarrel, legal dispute) — the legal register: do not open a case against someone without grounds. Ḥinnām (for nothing, without cause) — the gratuitous quality of the aggression. The repeated use of ḥinnām (also in 1:11) marks unmotivated harm as particularly condemned.
31: “Do not envy (qannēʾ) the violent person (ʾîš ḥāmās) or choose to follow any of his paths.” Qānāʾ (to be jealous, zealously desire — root of the divine epithet ʾēl qannāʾ, jealous God, Exod 20:5) — the covenantal emotion of jealousy / zeal turned toward the violent person’s apparent success. Ḥāmās (violence, injustice) is the word used for the wickedness that precipitated the flood (Gen 6:11–13). Envy of the violent is its own form of moral corruption.
32: “For the LORD detests (tôʿᵃbat-YHWH) the devious (naʿᵃlôz).” Tôʿēbāh (abomination, what is detested) is one of the strongest negative assessment terms in Proverbs — it appears 22 times in the book, marking the most offensive transgressions (cf. 6:16–19; 11:1; 12:22). Lûz (to deviate, be crooked — cf. 2:15) defines the devious person. “But the upright (yᵉšārîm) are in his confidence (sôdô).” Sôd (intimate counsel, secret circle — the divine council, Ps 25:14; Jer 23:18; Amos 3:7) — the upright are admitted to God’s inner circle.
33: “The LORD’s curse (mᵉʾērat-YHWH) rests on the house of the wicked, but he blesses (yᵉbārek) the home (nᵉwēh) of the righteous.” Mᵉʾērāh (curse) is the covenantal curse-word of Deuteronomy (Deut 28:20; Mal 2:2; 3:9). Nāweh (home, pasture, settled dwelling) suggests the home as a place of pastoral security. The contrast between cursed house and blessed home is the Deuteronomic two-ways applied to domestic life.
34: “He mocks the mockers (lēṣîm yālîṣ) himself, but shows favor (ḥēn) to the humble (ʿᵃnāwîm).” Lêṣ (mocker, scorner — cf. 1:22) is repaid in kind: God meets the scorner’s mockery with his own sovereign mockery (cf. Ps 2:4). ʿĀnāw (humble, afflicted, meek — the socially low and spiritually receptive) receives ḥēn (grace, favor). This verse is directly quoted in Jas 4:6 and 1 Pet 5:5 via the LXX, and is the fountainhead of the NT teaching on grace to the humble.
35: “The wise (ḥᵃkāmîm) will inherit (yinḥᵃlû) honor (kābôd), but fools (kᵉsîlîm) are left with disgrace (mārîm qālôn).” Nāḥal (to inherit as a lasting possession — the language of land-inheritance) applied to honor: wisdom’s honor is a permanent inheritance, not a temporary reputation. The contrast of kābôd (glory, weighty honor) with qālôn (shame, dishonor — the light and trivial) closes ch3’s positive portrait of wisdom with the two ultimate destinies: permanent honor or disgrace.
